Home · Tech · About

The Responsive Image Problem

By Pat Pixel

Modern news sites almost never put the real image URL in an image's src attribute. Instead they ship a tiny grey placeholder and list the actual, full-resolution photographs inside a picture element's source descriptors, letting the browser choose the right size for the viewport. It is an elegant solution for performance, and a quiet trap for any tool that only ever looks at the src attribute when deciding what to show a reader.

When a reading tool clones the page and hands it to an extractor, that placeholder src is all a naive pass sees. The remedy is to look where the real image actually lives: the image's own srcset, and failing that the source descriptors of the enclosing picture element, and only then the assorted data attributes that lazy-loading scripts leave behind for their own use.

The harder case is the genuinely empty placeholder, the image element that carries nothing but a spacer and never had a real source at all. Rendering it produces an ugly blank rectangle in the middle of otherwise clean prose, so the right move is simply to drop it once extraction has had every chance to fill it in.

Get both cases right and the reader shows real photographs where the author put them and nothing where the author left only scaffolding, which is exactly what a calm reading surface should do for the person who came to read rather than to debug someone else's markup.

Boilerplate that should not appear in the extracted article.
